match.title: Template-based RNA structure prediction advanced through a blind code competition
match.authorString: Lee Y, He S, Oda T, Rao GJ, Kim Y, Kim R, Kim H, Heng CK, Kowerko D, Li H, Nguyen H, Sampathkumar A, Enrique Gómez R, Chen M, Yoshizawa A, Kuraishi S, Ogawa K, Zou S, Paullier A, Zhao B, Chen H, Hsu T, Hirano T, Chiu W, Gezelle JG, Haack D, Hong Y, Jadhav S, Koirala D, Kretsch RC, Lewicka A, Li S, Marcia M, Piccirilli J, Rudolfs B, Srivastava Y, Steckelberg A, Su Z, Toor N, Wang L, Yang Z, Zhang K, Zou J, Baker D, Chen S, Demkin M, Favor A, Hummer AM, Joshi CK, Kryshtafovych A, Küçükbenli E, Miao Z, Moult J, Munley C, Reade W, Viel T, Westhof E, Zhang S, Das R.
match.abstractText: Automatically predicting RNA 3D structure from sequence remains an unsolved challenge in biology and biotechnology. Here, we describe a Kaggle code competition engaging over 1700 teams and 43 previously unreleased structures to tackle this challenge. The top three submitted algorithms achieved scores within statistical error of the winners of the recent CASP16 competition. Unexpectedly, the top Kaggle strategy involved a pipeline for discovering 3D templates, without the use of deep learning. We integrated this template-modeling pipeline and other Kaggle strategies to develop a single model RNAPro that retrospectively outperformed individual Kaggle models on the same test set. These results suggest a growing importance of template-based modeling in RNA structure prediction.

citations.title: Black-box data: a new paradigm for biomedicine in the AI era
The trRosettaRNA server for RNA structure prediction

narrative:
Short impact summary

This preprint presents a novel approach to RNA structure prediction, leveraging a competitive framework that enhances template-based methodologies. The work has generated interest evidenced by 2 citations in key journals such as Chemical Science and Nature Protocols.

Full narrative

The preprint titled "Template-based RNA structure prediction advanced through a blind code competition," hosted on bioRxiv, showcases significant advancements in RNA structure prediction methodologies. The authors, affiliated with prominent institutions including the Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence and Uppsala University, are driving innovation in this crucial area of biological research.

This research has already made its impact felt in the scientific community, with 2 citations recorded in high-impact journals such as Chemical Science and Nature Protocols, illustrating its relevance and adoption among researchers. The collaboration between institutions from various countries, including Sweden, France, and the United States, underscores a broad geographic footprint and collective investment in advancing RNA predictive technologies.

The work not only contributes academically but has also drawn public attention, as evidenced by a mention on Wikipedia. This inclusion highlights the relevance of this research to broader societal discussions about RNA applications, suggesting an interest that transcends traditional academic circles.

Additionally, the absence of funding agencies signals an independent research impetus, allowing for a focused exploration of RNA structure prediction without external financial bias. This can foster originality in the research approach, as the authors navigate the complexities of this scientific challenge without constraints imposed by funding priorities. Furthermore, the work has demonstrated proven downstream reuse, as it is already enabling subsequent research efforts, showcasing its utility and significance in pushing the boundaries of RNA structural biology.
